author: @Native_Creation
comment_text:
Good overview, but there's some other elements missing. The German Empire supported many Mexican factions, especially Villa's with arms and advisors (they trained them with the machine guns). Double and triple agents were working on the U.S. side of the border as well. After the Zimmerman Telegram, a lot of these agents lost their cover and Villa's support was weakened. The US not just backed, but controlled Porfirio Diaz for US interests.

There were also talks of Japan joining Mexico in the invasion of the US, but that fizzled out early when it got leaked. Highly recommend the film "Pancho Villa Starring Himself" which is based on the real life account of Raoul Walsh filming Villa's campaign, which is considered the very first war documentary.

A lot of people here in the border, including my own family, say we're related to Pancho Villa, or that they saw him when he visited the towns along the border. He got around.

author: @deltharion
comment_text:
This documentary is a very good improvement on the previous one. Thank God you dropped the "bandits turn revolutionaries" title because the only major bandit who turned revolutionary was Villa lol.
Anyway, I’d like to add a few things:
Zapata was also a landowner, not exactly a peasant: he was somewhat in a "middle class" status, more similar to Madero rather than the peasantry. However, he became very close to his fellow Morelian peasants because of how much he was subjected to abuse by the large landowning classes. The general narrative of the revolution always centers on the peasantry, but the core of the entire conflict was a dispute of interests between elites: Díaz and his old order against the merchant oligarchy, the middle class, and the emerging young upper class, who argued that there were too many obstacles preventing them from accessing politics. Because of this, they turned against the old elites and together used the peasants as cannon fodder, employing the social narrative to legitimize themselves.
That being said, given how massive the conflict was, the revolution gradually granted the concessions, rights, and freedoms that it had promised to the peasantry and the lower classes, especially during the Cárdenas government.
And well, most of the peasantry joined these movements as an immediate reaction to the resentment they had toward their elites and local authorities. Their vision of what the federal government should be was not something they had established. The situation throughout Mexico was mixed: there were places where what was mentioned in the video was a common thing, such as Morelos or Chihuahua, where the local governments expropriated land and sold it to the hacendados. But there were also places where the dynamics were different—where there was no massive conflict at all, and where it did, it was for reasons unrelated to those generally attributed to the revolution. For example, the rebels in Baja California simply wanted greater political representation in the federal government.
Going back to Zapata, here are two things i want to add:
He never asked the Bolsheviks for help. He only published a text in which he praised them as liberators of the Russian people and stated that both movements should be in solidarity with each other. However, this was more of a formal statement than a literal request for aid.
The term "Zapata Commune" is a concept coined by Marxist historian Adolfo Gilly in 1971, and unfortunately, it has distorted things quite a bit because Zapata was not a socialist. He was a tenant landowner dissatisfied with the land situation in his state, and his goal was agrarian reform and better treatment for the peasantry—nothing more.
The Plan de Ayala never demanded a radical collectivization of land or communes. It only required that people bring their titles granted by the Spanish Crown or the Mexican government before the Ley Lerdo, so their former lands could be returned to them. Regarding the land that remained with the haciendas, it only required that one-third of the land be ceded—as long as they cooperated with the plan. If they refused, then two-thirds of their land would be used to compensate the widows of the revolution. Not very socialist tbh.

Nothing was said about most inspiring war or conflict. Has to do with:
"Viva Cristo Rey” is a battle cry that translates to “Long Live Christ the King!”. It was used by the Cristeros, a group of people who fought in the Cristero Rebellion, a conflict between the Mexican state and the Catholic Church that took place from 1926 to 1929. The rebellion was a result of the 1917 Constitution's anticlerical and secularist articles. 
There was a movie about it. It highly infers that the Free Masons had a major role or actual perpetrators of the revolution.

author: @jjeherrera
comment_text: A very complex process to squeeze in a 20 min. video, but it covered the essential issues. For a more extensive account I recommend "The Secret War in Mexico" by Friedrich Katz, which shows how much international intervention shaped the Mexican Revolution, since the fall of Porfirio Díaz to the murder of Carranza. It also explains how the invasion of Veracruz by the American Navy attempted and failed to stop the supply of German weapons to Huerta. What is here described as skirmishes between the American punitive expedition and Carranza's forces was actually a full scale battle at Carrizales, which swept an American regiment, and forced the withdrawal of the expedition. One may wonder why Woodrow Wilson didn't order a full scale invasion after that, but that's obviously what Germany would have liked. There were more important business to attend in Europe.

author: @bernardolopezpina7211
comment_text:
Huerta gained Madero’s trust by suppressing Orozco’s rebellion against Madero.

When Huerta took power, he did so on a technicality: Madero and Pino Suárez (president and vice president) were forced to resign, the next in line, Foreign Minister Lascurain, took office for 45 minutes and was strong armed into designating Huerta as the next in line.

Huerta then sent a telegram to every state governor announcing: “Madero and Pino Suárez were made prisoners, I’ve been designated President by the Senate”.

In light of such travesty, Governor Carranza of Madero’s home state of Coahuila urged the State’s Congress to issue a decree openly declaring a rebellion on Huerta on February 19th, 1913.

author: @deltharion
comment_text:
This documentary is a very good improvement on the previous one. Thank God you dropped the "bandits turn revolutionaries" title because the only major bandit who turned revolutionary was Villa lol.
Anyway, I’d like to add a few things:
Zapata was also a landowner, not exactly a peasant: he was somewhat in a "middle class" status, more similar to Madero rather than the peasantry. However, he became very close to his fellow Morelian peasants because of how much he was subjected to abuse by the large landowning classes. The general narrative of the revolution always centers on the peasantry, but the core of the entire conflict was a dispute of interests between elites: Díaz and his old order against the merchant oligarchy, the middle class, and the emerging young upper class, who argued that there were too many obstacles preventing them from accessing politics. Because of this, they turned against the old elites and together used the peasants as cannon fodder, employing the social narrative to legitimize themselves.
That being said, given how massive the conflict was, the revolution gradually granted the concessions, rights, and freedoms that it had promised to the peasantry and the lower classes, especially during the Cárdenas government.
And well, most of the peasantry joined these movements as an immediate reaction to the resentment they had toward their elites and local authorities. Their vision of what the federal government should be was not something they had established. The situation throughout Mexico was mixed: there were places where what was mentioned in the video was a common thing, such as Morelos or Chihuahua, where the local governments expropriated land and sold it to the hacendados. But there were also places where the dynamics were different—where there was no massive conflict at all, and where it did, it was for reasons unrelated to those generally attributed to the revolution. For example, the rebels in Baja California simply wanted greater political representation in the federal government.
Going back to Zapata, here are two things i want to add:
He never asked the Bolsheviks for help. He only published a text in which he praised them as liberators of the Russian people and stated that both movements should be in solidarity with each other. However, this was more of a formal statement than a literal request for aid.
The term "Zapata Commune" is a concept coined by Marxist historian Adolfo Gilly in 1971, and unfortunately, it has distorted things quite a bit because Zapata was not a socialist. He was a tenant landowner dissatisfied with the land situation in his state, and his goal was agrarian reform and better treatment for the peasantry—nothing more.
The Plan de Ayala never demanded a radical collectivization of land or communes. It only required that people bring their titles granted by the Spanish Crown or the Mexican government before the Ley Lerdo, so their former lands could be returned to them. Regarding the land that remained with the haciendas, it only required that one-third of the land be ceded—as long as they cooperated with the plan. If they refused, then two-thirds of their land would be used to compensate the widows of the revolution. Not very socialist tbh.

comment_text:
Nothing was said about most inspiring war or conflict. Has to do with:
"Viva Cristo Rey” is a battle cry that translates to “Long Live Christ the King!”. It was used by the Cristeros, a group of people who fought in the Cristero Rebellion, a conflict between the Mexican state and the Catholic Church that took place from 1926 to 1929. The rebellion was a result of the 1917 Constitution's anticlerical and secularist articles. 
There was a movie about it. It highly infers that the Free Masons had a major role or actual perpetrators of the revolution.
If you think you want to report honestly of such happenings you have to say it right, not neglect most crucial link of history and information. The Free Masons wanted to control Mexico and eliminate the Catholic Church.

author: @jjeherrera
comment_text: A very complex process to squeeze in a 20 min. video, but it covered the essential issues. For a more extensive account I recommend "The Secret War in Mexico" by Friedrich Katz, which shows how much international intervention shaped the Mexican Revolution, since the fall of Porfirio Díaz to the murder of Carranza. It also explains how the invasion of Veracruz by the American Navy attempted and failed to stop the supply of German weapons to Huerta. What is here described as skirmishes between the American punitive expedition and Carranza's forces was actually a full scale battle at Carrizales, which swept an American regiment, and forced the withdrawal of the expedition. One may wonder why Woodrow Wilson didn't order a full scale invasion after that, but that's obviously what Germany would have liked. There were more important business to attend in Europe.

author: @bernardolopezpina7211
comment_text:
Huerta gained Madero’s trust by suppressing Orozco’s rebellion against Madero.

When Huerta took power, he did so on a technicality: Madero and Pino Suárez (president and vice president) were forced to resign, the next in line, Foreign Minister Lascurain, took office for 45 minutes and was strong armed into designating Huerta as the next in line.

Huerta then sent a telegram to every state governor announcing: “Madero and Pino Suárez were made prisoners, I’ve been designated President by the Senate”.

In light of such travesty, Governor Carranza of Madero’s home state of Coahuila urged the State’s Congress to issue a decree openly declaring a rebellion on Huerta on February 19th, 1913.

author: @WillN2Go1
comment_text: I've always wondered about Villa's use of trains. In John Reed's Mexico on Fire he mentions numerous battles where Villa moved his army on trains distances to surprise his adversary. Seemed highly effective. While reading T.E. Lawrence Seven Pillars of Wisdom talking about roughly the same time period, the Arab Revolt was blowing up Turkish trains. I suspect the tactical use of trains diminished somewhat because of this vulnerability. (Though the Red Army used them during the Russian Civil War. Did the Whites blow up any Red Army trains?)  After that it seemed trains still had an important role but behind the lines.  What's critical to understanding what happened in Mexico during this period is the two to three million deaths many of them civilians.  When I was a teacher 15 years ago I was told about a couple of very old veterans of Villa's army  in northeast Los Angeles near the school. Likely they were already deceased. Viva Mexico!
